flashcards.front: What is object assignment in C++?
flashcards.back: Object assignment is the process of copying the contents of one object to another using the assignment operator (=). By default, C++ performs a member-wise (shallow) copy where each data member is copied directly.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between copy constructor and assignment operator?
flashcards.back: Copy constructor is called when a new object is created from an existing object. Assignment operator is called when an already existing object is assigned a new value.

Shallow copy copies only the member values (pointers store addresses). If a class contains pointers to dynamic memory, both objects will point to the same memory, causing potential double-deletion issues.
Deep copy creates a complete independent copy of an object including duplicating the memory that pointers point to. It requires a user-defined copy constructor and assignment operator.

flashcards.front: What is the Rule of Three in C++?
flashcards.back: If a class requires a user-defined destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator, it likely needs all three to properly manage resources like dynamic memory.

flashcards.front: What is the Rule of Five in C++?
flashcards.back: In C++11+, the Rule of Five includes: destructor, copy constructor, move constructor, copy assignment operator, and move assignment operator. If you define any, you should define all five.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'this' pointer?
flashcards.back: The 'this' pointer is an implicit pointer that points to the current object instance. It is used to differentiate between object members and parameters with the same name.

flashcards.front: What is operator overloading for assignment?
flashcards.back: It allows custom behavior when the assignment operator (=) is used with class objects. The assignment operator can be overloaded as a member function to control how objects are copied.

flashcards.front: What happens if assignment operator is not defined?
flashcards.back: If not defined, the compiler generates a default copy assignment operator that performs member-wise shallow copy, which may cause issues with dynamically allocated memory.

flashcards.front: What is self-assignment and why handle it?
flashcards.back: Self-assignment occurs when an object is assigned to itself (a = a). A well-written assignment operator should check for self-assignment to avoid unnecessary work and potential memory corruption.
Copy elision is a compiler optimization that removes redundant copy/move operations, allowing objects to be constructed directly in their destination without copying.

flashcards.front: What is the signature of copy assignment operator?
flashcards.back: ClassName& operator=(const ClassName& obj). It returns a reference to allow chaining (a = b = c) and takes a const reference parameter.

flashcards.front: Why return reference from assignment operator?
flashcards.back: Returning reference enables assignment chaining (a = b = c = d) and allows expressions like (a = b) to have the value of a, following standard C conventions.
Member-wise assignment copies each data member from the source object to the corresponding member of the destination object, performed automatically by the default assignment operator.

flashcards.front: Why use const in assignment operator parameter?
flashcards.back: Using const ensures the source object is not modified during assignment, allows passing both const and non-const objects, and follows best practices for non-modifying operations.
